---
title: "Pilgrimage"
date: 2026-07-22
---

There’s a very specific kind of movie I love; grounded, cool, and kinda... green.

Take Maara and Vaayai Moodi Pesavum, for example. I crave stories like those. Why? Because they’re so grounded. When a scene takes place somewhere, it feels like the characters actually feel the space. No matter how huge the setting is, it always feels small, cozy, and compact like home.

On top of that, the characters are actual characters. They’re distinct, flawed, and carry their own personalities and personal histories; they aren’t just sitting there as props to serve the protagonist or main plot. Unironically, their role feels delightfully gender-unspecific. Sure, they have a defined gender, but you could swap it tomorrow and the story would still work beautifully.

Most importantly, there isn't just one narrative running. We aren't rigidly orbiting a single main plot. Instead, there are multiple storylines happening in parallel; some start early, some start late, some finish quickly, and others don't really "end" at all, yet still leave you with a satisfying sense of closure.

Unlike typical commercial blockbusters that obsess over one protagonist and a single linear plot, a film like Vaayai Moodi Pesavum has at least four side stories converging into one. Those side plots don't even exist to aid the main story—they just make the world feel richer, you know? No single person holds the fate of that universe, yet everyone feels important. That balance creates an innate groundedness and scratches a niche itch I didn't even realize I had.

I had this exact same feeling watching Trying on Apple TV a year ago, and I fell head over heels for it. Everyone in that show has their own distinct persona, struggles, and charm, and the series weaves all their lives together seamlessly. It plays with such poetic contrasts: the poorest family is the kindest and happiest, the richest family is the loneliest, the oldest man works the hardest, and the youngsters are the lazy ones...

Are these 10/10 masterpieces at storytelling? Maybe not, but I still adore them. *Things doesn't have to be a masterwork for you to fall completely in love with it.*
